text: Converting CO2 to fuel and chemicals is considered one of the strategies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. However, the high thermodynamic stability of CO2 molecules leads to large activation energy barriers, and so catalysts are needed. It has been shown that using nanoparticles of certain metals, particularly gold, on appropriate oxide supports can promote high catalytic activities. It was reported that the bulk gold metal was not catalytically active, while it could act as an active catalyst when it was used as nanoparticles supported on particular oxides. Using experiments and simulations based on density functional theory, the activity of Au nanoparticles supported on a mixed-oxide material of cerium and titanium for catalytic conversion of CO2 has been demonstrated. It has been revealed that Au nanoparticles anchored on CeO x/TiO2 were catalytically active and thereby facilitate the reduction of CO2 by hydrogen at relatively low pressures. A novel explanation is provided where a highly polarizing metal like Au induces an electronic polarization effect on a oxide material that in turn yields favorable adsorption sites for the CO2 molecules and catalytic activity toward reduction of CO2 into hydrocarbons at relatively low pressure, in particular, to form preferentially the desired product of methanol in greater yield than that of Cu catalysts.
explanation: The text reads as a plausible, domain-specific summary with coherent structure and technical terms, but a minor grammar slip ('on a oxide material') and slightly generic phrasing suggest it could be human-written; it is not conclusive evidence of AI authorship.
